exemplar.title: To what extent does the area of Corso Buenos Aires in Milan suffer urban stress with proximity to the CBD?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does the area of Corso Buenos Aires in Milan suffer urban stress with proximity to the CBD?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Research question is explicitly geographical, linked to IB theory, and allows hypothesis formulation.
Precise site description with quantified distance from the CBD and clear feasibility for primary data collection.
weaknesses: Maps lack full adherence to conventions (missing scale bars and north arrows on some figures).
Operational definitions (e.g., âurban stressâ) could be more explicitly defined.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The criterion shows excellence in question formulation and context: the question is clearly geographical with theoretical links (Band 3) and the location is specified in detail (Band 3). Mapping conventions are inconsistently applied, dropping one strand to Band 2. On balance, two strands meet the highest descriptors and one is mid-band, warranting the top holistic mark of 3 out of 3, reflecting strong geographic context and question formulation despite minor mapping convention issues.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods are clearly described and linked to each hypothesis (systematic sampling, rental data normalization).
Data collection techniques were correctly implemented (consistent readings, transparent processes).
weaknesses: Justification for sampling design (intervals, temporal choices) is thin.
Sample sizes are modest, limiting data quality and reliability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands sit firmly in the Band 2 descriptor: methods are described with clear relevance and techniques correctly used but with minimal data collection. The lack of theoretical justification for sampling design and modest sample sizes prevent a Band 3 award, so 2 out of 3 is appropriate.

strengths: Data sets are directly relevant to the research question across multiple measures (rent, noise, litter).
Appropriate presentation techniques (line graphs, bar charts, annotated maps) clearly convey results.
Conventions (titles, figure numbers, sources) are generally adhered to, with only occasional errors.
weaknesses: Gaps in data sufficiency (limited temporal coverage, missing pedestrian counts).
Presentation could be enhanced with more advanced techniques (scatterplots with trend lines, confidence intervals).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates relevant and sufficient data, appropriate presentation methods, and mostly correct conventions, situating it at the upper end of the 3â4 band. While more effective techniques would improve clarity, the consistency and relevance of information justify 4 out of 6, reflecting solid treatment with minor gaps.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Use of descriptive (mean, median, standard deviation) and basic inferential (Spearmanâs Ï) statistics.
Trends and anomalies are identified and linked to bid-rent/urban stress theory.
Each hypothesis is addressed in the analysis.
weaknesses: Interpretation of Spearmanâs rho contradicts plotted trends and lacks confidence levels.
Some data inconsistencies (distance reporting) and gaps (missing pedestrian data) remain.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analytical techniques go beyond basic description and include inferential statistics, placing the work in the 5â6 band, but inconsistent interpretation and missing confidence measures limit its reach. Similarly, data interpretation is linked to theory but sometimes superficial. Overall, the analysis falls into the lower half of the 5â6 band, justifying 4 out of 8.

strengths: The conclusion restates main findings and links back to all three hypotheses.
weaknesses: Support is weakened by inconsistencies in statistical interpretation (Spearmanâs results).
Contradictions between conclusion statements and earlier analysis reduce clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion provides a summary and links to hypotheses, meeting the Band 1 descriptor for a partially supported conclusion. Analytical inconsistencies prevent full support, so the mark is 1 out of 2.

strengths: Evaluation identifies clear strengths and limitations of methodology (systematic sampling, seasonal data).
Improvement suggestions are relevant (expanding sample size, refining data sources).
The impact of limitations on data reliability is explained.
weaknesses: Suggestions could include more detailed discussion of how each improvement would enhance validity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation goes beyond listing to explain relevant strengths, weaknesses, and the effects on results, and outlines improvement suggestions with rationale. This aligns with the Band 3 descriptor for a comprehensive and relevant evaluation, justifying 3 out of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two maps are provided (hand-drawn Milan map, regional map, CBD map). They include north arrows/scale on some but not all, and the hand-drawn map lacks coordinate precision. While the maps do locate the study area, conventions are inconsistently applied and detail is sometimes excessive or blurred, matching the mid-band descriptor rather than the top band.

The research question is explicitly geographical, focused on urban stress along a clearly-named street, and is explicitly linked to IB Option G and the bid-rent/Burgess theoretical frameworks. Three testable hypotheses flow directly from it, demonstrating that the question is framed so hypotheses can be generated. This aligns with the top-band descriptor.

Line graphs, bar charts and mapped locations are appropriate and clear, but alternatives such as scatterplots with correlation lines for rental price vs. distance are absent, and axes sometimes lack units. Hence techniques are âappropriateâ rather than âmost effectiveâ, meriting the 3-4 band with a score of 3.

Techniques were implemented correctly (prices normalised, repeated noise readings, manual counts) yet the sample sizes are modest (5 noise sites, 3â4 shop rents per block) and only two temporal readings. Reliability is acceptable but not high-quality. This aligns with band 2: âtechniques correctly used but with minimal data collectionâ.

The student calculates mean, median, standard deviation and Spearmanâs Ï, showing appropriate descriptive and basic inferential statistics. However, the Spearman interpretation contradicts the plotted trend and confidence levels are absent. This corresponds to the 3-4 band with a mid-level score.

Trends and some anomalies are identified and linked to bid-rent and urban stress theory, but explanations are sometimes superficial (e.g., Block 6 rental peak mis-located relative to CBD) and contradictory statements on noise correlation appear. This matches the 3-4 band, lower end.

The student identifies a single 1.2 km street, specifies each block, and explains why the site is appropriate for primary data collection. The CBD reference point (Duomo) is quantified (2 km) and feasibility of fieldwork is evident from the photographs and timing table, satisfying the highest descriptor for locational specificity.

The evaluation lists clear strengths (systematic sampling, seasonal data) and limitations (rain, uncooperative agent) with brief explanation of how these affected results. This matches an âappropriate outlineâ, i.e., band 2.

All data sets relate directly to urban stress, but the noise sample and temporal coverage are limited, and some gaps exist (no pedestrian counts). Thus data are largely, though not fully, sufficientâplacing the work at the lower end of the 3-4 band.

The conclusion restates main findings and links back to all three hypotheses, but support is weakened by earlier analytical inconsistencies (e.g., misinterpreted Spearman results). Therefore it is only âpartially supportedâ, fitting the 1-mark band.

Each hypothesis is analysed, yet there are evident gaps: pedestrian counts are missing, and seasonal comparisons are limited to a single extra reading day. Thus analysis is present but incomplete, aligning with the descriptor âanalysis with some gapsâ (3-4 band, 3 marks).

The methods (systematic sampling per block, use of online rental portals, sound-meter app, manual litter count) are described and linked to each hypothesis, but explanations are brief and theoretical justification is thin. For example, sampling interval rationale and statistical tests choice are not fully explained. This fits the âdescribed with clear relevanceâ band.

Figure numbers, titles and sources are generally present, but several captions have typographical errors (Figure #3, â¬ symbol) and some graphs omit axis units. These are âoccasional errorsâ, so the strand sits in the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

Suggestions (use only online sources, enlarge sample area/size) are relevant but lack detailed discussion of how they would improve reliability or validity. This fits band 2: ârelevant improvement suggestions outlinedâ.

annotations.comment: The research question is explicitly geographical, linked to IB Option G and theoretical models, and facilitates hypothesis development effectively.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The streetâlevel image lacks a north arrow and scale bar, which are essential conventions for clear spatial representation on maps.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Define and operationalize âurban stressâ explicitly to improve clarity and allow more precise measurement in the investigation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The list of figures identifies useful maps but does not note if coordinate grids or scale bars are included, limiting interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Context description provides precise locational details and quantifies distance from the CBD, clearly demonstrating feasibility for primary data collection.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The geographical context paragraph relies heavily on secondary sources without critically evaluating or linking them to observed field conditions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figure 1 map lacks a visible scale bar, which hinders accurate distance interpretation along Corso Buenos Aires.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 2âs regional map does not include a north arrow, reducing its clarity and adherence to mapping conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Background history details are thorough but could be more directly linked to urban stress concepts used later in the analysis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The hypotheses list is clearly structured but would benefit from justification of the sampling design rationale.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Hypothesis 1 description effectively links Burgess and BidâRent theory to spatial variation in rental cost.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Use of literature to justify expected noise levels demonstrates good engagement with secondary studies.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The narrative for Hypothesis 3 ends mid-sentence, lacking a full statement of expected outcomes.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Methodology outlines systematic sampling but does not justify temporal choices or sampling frequency.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Rental data sourcing and normalization process are described clearly, ensuring transparency of the technique.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The formula for standard deviation is correctly applied, but specify whether this is a sample or population calculation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The final standard deviation result is stated without the unit (â¬), which should be included for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Noise data were collected at only five locations; consider expanding sites or adding pedestrian counts to strengthen reliability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Annotated screenshots of the Sound Meter app are well presented and clarify data collection procedures.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figures lack legends or scale indicators on the block map, reducing precision in identifying sampling positions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Manual cigarette count method is clearly described and captures spatial trends effectively.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Use scatterplots with fitted lines to present correlation between rental prices and distance, enhancing analytical depth.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The line graph interpretation identifies trends but omits calculation of correlation coefficient or statistical significance.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Distance values for Block 6 and others are inconsistent with earlier methodology; ensure uniform distance reporting.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Include error bars or confidence intervals in the noise pollution graph to reflect the variability of repeated measurements.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Spearmanâs rho interpretation contradicts the plotted trend; revisit the statistical test or justify anomalous results clearly.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Cigarette butts analysis notes anomalies but does not link these findings back to theoretical frameworks like bid-rent.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: The bar chart lacks axis units for frequency, which makes it difficult to interpret the magnitude of litter counts.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The conclusion restates results well but does not address contradictions in noise data interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion E: Conclusion

annotations.comment: Conclusion reports Spearman correlations ($r_s=-0.9$, $-1$) that contradict earlier discussion; ensure consistency or clarify discrepancies.
annotations.groupName: Criterion E: Conclusion

annotations.comment: Evaluation highlights methodological tools but would be strengthened by deeper critique of specific procedure limitations.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: Improvement suggestions mention sample enlargement but lack explicit discussion of how this increases validity and reliability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation
